metadata.title: Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations: Insights and implications

abstract:
To comprehensively analyze and further enhance the established E4 communication model for online medical counseling in Chinese settings, by proposing the novel E5 model. Additionally, it aims to evaluate the performance of Chinese doctors in fulfilling the E5 model. Methods: Through thematic analysis and grounded theory of 500 online medical consultations in China, we developed the extended E5 model from the E4 model. We identified four dimensions of patient attitudes and behaviors using Stanford Topic Modeling Toolbox, then employed Chi-square analysis to investigate their influence on doctors' performance of E5 model. Results: Our study illustrates that the extended E5 model, with its operable strategies, accurately mirrors the nuanced dynamics of online medical counseling in China, significantly varying in doctors' execution in response to the four identified dimensions of patient attitudes and behaviors.

The extended E5 model, coupled with insights into patient attitudes and behaviors, provides a comprehensive framework for understanding and enhancing communication in China's online healthcare context. Practice implications: The findings highlight the necessity for doctor training in the E5 model for effective online communication. Furthermore, fostering conducive relationship between patients and doctors could potentially boost doctors' E5 performance.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: The proliferation of online medical consultation has drastically transformed the healthcare landscape, giving patients greater access to health services and information. In China, the increased use of online consultations is especially important due to the country's low physicianto-patient ratio, a situation that was made worse during the COVID-19 pandemic. The Chinese government has implemented strict measures like quarantining infected individuals and their close contacts, as well as closing public venues, to reduce the spread of the virus and lessen the impact of the pandemic. Amid this milieu, online medical consultation services became a crucial alternative for Chinese citizens, offering medical support without spatio-temporal limitations [1] and alleviating the pressure on China's offline hospitals. With the digital shift in healthcare, effective verbal communication becomes even more important for medical counseling. Therefore, ensuring robust and effective communication in online consultations is crucial for improving healthcare experiences and promoting the sustainable development of online services in China. In the latter half of the 20th century, medical consultations were revolutionized by advances in clinical, health, and social psychology, as well as cognitive and behavioral paradigms [2]. The works of Berne's [3] and Heron [4] laid the groundwork for understanding the complex dynamics of doctor-patient interactions, highlighting the critical role that effective communication plays in medical outcomes. One influential advocate was George Engel, who introduced the biopsychosocial model in 1977, challenging the prevailing biomedical model of medicine and advocating for a more holistic and patient-centered approach [5]. Engel's biopsychosocial model posits that health and illness are the results of a complex interplay between biological, psychological, and social factors, rather than being solely based on biological conditions [5]. This paradigm-shifting model laid the conceptual foundation for patient-centered communication which prioritizes patients' needs, values, and preferences while encouraging open dialogue, shared decision-making, and mutual respect between healthcare providers and patients [6,7].

Building upon Engel's conceptual foundation, several key principles have come to define the landscape of patient-centered communication. Shared decision-making stands as a cornerstone, advocating a collaborative approach to healthcare choices that honors not only empirical evidence but also the patient's own values and preferences [8][9][10]. Another critical element is empathy, which enables healthcare providers to better understand and respond to the emotional and psychological needs of patients, thereby enhancing patient satisfaction and potentially improving health outcomes [11][12][13]. Active listening serves as an instrumental skill, empowering providers to fully engage with their patients' concerns, leading to more accurate diagnoses and more effective treatment plans [14]. Trust forms the underpinning of these interactions, establishing a safe space for honest dialogue, improved adherence to medical advice, and higher overall satisfaction with healthcare services [15]. Lastly, holistic care encapsulates the need to view patients as complex beings influenced by a myriad of biological, psychological, and social factors, demanding a treatment approach that acknowledges this complexity [16]. Collectively, these principles shape a framework for patient-centered communication, designed to foster mutual respect, open dialogue, and ultimately, better healthcare outcomes.

Various communication models have also been formulated to facilitate patient-centered communication in medical settings [17][18][19][20][21]. Among the most prominent is the E4 model which emerged as an indispensable framework in healthcare communication research, particularly concerning patient-doctor dialogues. The model encapsulates the key dimensions of Engage, Empathize, Educate, and Enlist, offering a comprehensive lens through which to assess and optimize medical interactions [22]. Its multi-dimensional approach has garnered empirical validation, as evidenced by its application in various clinical settings to elevate the quality of healthcare consultations [20,23,24]. Numerous investigations have deployed the E4 model as a tool to evaluate healthcare professionals' communication proficiencies, demonstrating its utility in enhancing both patient satisfaction and clinical outcomes [25][26][27]. Therefore, the E4 model's robustness, empirical support, and broad applicability make it an ideal theoretical cornerstone for examining healthcare communication across diverse contexts.

Building on these theoretical foundations, our study zooms in on several key constructs that serve as the lens through which we analyze online medical consultations in China. These constructs include: 1) Communication Models, specifically the E4 model adapted to China's unique healthcare landscape; 2) Patient Attitudes and Behaviors, such as politeness, informed literacy, anxiety expression, and trust, which are posited to influence the dynamics of online consultations; and 3) Strategy Utilization, examining the frequency and effectiveness of specific communicative strategies employed by healthcare providers in online settings. These constructs will be systematically examined using discourse analysis and grounded theory approaches, enabling a robust evaluation of the existing communication paradigms in China's rapidly growing digital healthcare domain.

With the rise of digital healthcare, the patient-centered approach has gained even greater significance. Online consultations have increased accessibility and convenience [28], extending patient-centered care to diverse populations. Nonetheless, the virtual nature of these consultations presents unique challenges [29], such as the potential misinterpretation of non-verbal cues of empathetic communication [30]. Despite these challenges, research suggests that the principles of patient-centered care remain viable in online settings. Studies on online cognitive behavioral therapy suggest that therapeutic alliancesa fundamental component of patient-centered care-can be successfully established even in the absence of physical interactions [31]. Similarly, video consultations, despite lacking physical presence, can still facilitate a degree of personal connection and understanding [32].

China's healthcare landscape, heavily influenced by rapid urbanization, demographic shifts, and healthcare reforms [33], provides a unique context for examining patient-centered principles. Historically, China's healthcare system has operated on a paternalistic model, where doctors wielding substantial authority, often at the expense of patient participation [34]. Recent policy directives and health reforms have highlighted the importance of adopting a patient-centered approach [35][36][37][38], despite cultural, systemic, and educational barriers [39,40]. Notwithstanding these challenges, some promising advancements have been made in specific contexts. For example, a study by Zhou et al. [41] demonstrated that most inpatients from urban areas were satisfied with patient-centered care provided in public healthcare system. In addition, studies conducted in primary care settings indicates a positive correlation between doctors' patient-centered attitudes and higher levels of patient satisfaction [42].

Simultaneously, China has witnessed an unprecedented expansion in the use of online medical consultations [43], largely driven by the country's growing digital economy and recent public health crises. The popularity of online medical consultations can be attributed to their convenience, accessibility, and the potential to ease the burden on China's strained healthcare system [44]. However, while existing research has delved into aspects such as utilization, patient satisfaction, and quality of care [45][46][47][48][49], the integration of patient-centered care in online consultations remains a largely uncharted territory. This presents an opportunity for further investigation into how principles of patient-centered care can be applied and optimized in the context of online medical consultations in China. Given this context, this study aims to fill the existing gap in the literature by examining the communication model used in Chinese online consultations and proposing strategies for improving its effectiveness. We leverage data from one of China's largest online medical consultation platforms (www.haodf.com) to identify the current online communication model and examine Chinese doctors' performance of the patient-centered approach in online settings. By doing so, we aim to elucidate the communication model in Chinese online consultations, improve its effectiveness, and optimize the patient-centered approach in this digital setting. Such insights could potentially guide the evolution of online consultation platforms in China. Specifically, our study addresses the following research questions using discourse analysis and grounded theory approaches:
1, What communication model is currently employed in online medical consultations in China?
2, How do patient attitudes and behaviors impact dynamics of doctor-patient interaction in online consultations?

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: The data of this study comes from the MedDialog-CN [52]. The reason for choosing this dataset is threefold. Firstly, the dataset contains 1.1 million dialogues and 4 million utterances, scraped from the largest Chinese online consultation website (haodf.com) and believed to be the largest Chinese online medical dialogue dataset. Secondly, almost all specialties (29 broad categories of specialties and 172 fine-grained specialties) in medicine are covered in the dataset, including internal medicine, family medicine, and a wide variety of diseases (172 diseases), such as cancer and pneumonia. Thirdly, the patients in MedDialog-CN are from 31 provincial-level administrative divisions in China, with various ethics, age, gender, occupation, education, and income [52].

We analyzed all the collected doctor-patient dialogues in 2020, the year when COVID-19 began to spread nationwide in China. Next, we manually filtered out unfinished consultation dialogues. 1 A total of 500 online doctor-patient consultations from 2020 were randomly selected as the data for the present study. This sampling method was chosen to ensure a wide range of dialogues and maintain the representativeness of the population while minimizing bias. With respect to ethical concerns, Haodifu (haodf.com) provides legal anonymity to patients during online medical consultations, ensuring the protection of their identity. 2 Patients can thus receive online medical assistance without disclosing their identities publicly. Our study exclusively investigates communication models utilized in online medical consultations and does not entail any connection to specific patient identity information. Hence, formal consent acquisition was deemed unnecessary.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: We commenced with an extensive review of 500 dialogues from the MedDialog-CN dataset [52] to gain a comprehensive understanding of the dominant communication dynamics. Recognizing the potential of the E4 model to encapsulate these dynamics, we adopted it as our primary theoretical framework. However, given China's unique landscape of online medical consultations, we expanded the model by introducing a fifth 'E' (Enable), resulting in a more context-specific E5 model. This extension was accomplished through a methodological synthesis of grounded theory and deductive thematic analysis.

Our approach primarily aligned with deductive thematic analysis, leaning heavily on the theoretical grounding of the E4 model. We also incorporated grounded theory to ensure that our model accurately reflected the unique dynamics of online medical counseling in China. The resultant E5 model, consisting of Engage, Empathize, Educate, Enlist, and Enable, captures the nuances of doctor-patient communication in China's online healthcare context. During a more detailed analysis phase, specific strategies corresponding to each 'E' were identified and systematically categorized using NVivo (Version 12). This process resulted in the development of a tailored E5 model for effective communication in Chinese online medical counseling. Subsequently, we evaluated the extent to which Chinese doctors adhered to the strategies outlined in the E5 model, identifying underperforming areas. The evaluations primarily relied on the frequency of strategy utilization within the corresponding tasks.

In response to these findings, we further tested if the doctor's performances of the E5 were regulated by the patient's attitude or behaviors. Using the Stanford Topic Modeling Toolbox (Version 0.2.1), we identified four dimensions of patient attitude and behavior: 'Polite Patient Engagement' emerged through courteous patient communication and attempts to build rapport; 'Informed Patient Literacy' was evident in patients demonstrating an understanding of their health conditions and medication; 'Patient Anxiety Expression' captured instances where patients articulated their worries or fears; and 'Patient Trust and Adherence' was identified through patients' trust in medical advice and expressed intent to follow prescribed treatments. These dimensions served as variables in our subsequent analysis, enabling us to explore how patient attitudes and behaviors potentially influence doctors' efficacy in executing the E5 tasks. This bifocal analysis provides a nuanced understanding of the dynamics of doctor-patient interactions.

During the open coding process, two coders independently conducted the first round of coding. Subsequent coding iterations, guided by an iterative tagging approach, involved systematic revisions and refinements of the initial codes. Any coding discrepancies were resolved through case-by-case discussions, ensuring the emergence of nuanced themes that accurately represented the data. sections.text:
We commenced with an extensive review of 500 dialogues from the MedDialog-CN dataset [52] to gain a comprehensive understanding of the dominant communication dynamics. Recognizing the potential of the E4 model to encapsulate these dynamics, we adopted it as our primary theoretical framework. However, given China's unique landscape of online medical consultations, we expanded the model by introducing a fifth 'E' (Enable), resulting in a more context-specific E5 model. This extension was accomplished through a methodological synthesis of grounded theory and deductive thematic analysis.

Our approach primarily aligned with deductive thematic analysis, leaning heavily on the theoretical grounding of the E4 model. We also incorporated grounded theory to ensure that our model accurately reflected the unique dynamics of online medical counseling in China. The resultant E5 model, consisting of Engage, Empathize, Educate, Enlist, and Enable, captures the nuances of doctor-patient communication in China's online healthcare context. During a more detailed analysis phase, specific strategies corresponding to each 'E' were identified and systematically categorized using NVivo (Version 12). This process resulted in the development of a tailored E5 model for effective communication in Chinese online medical counseling. Subsequently, we evaluated the extent to which Chinese doctors adhered to the strategies outlined in the E5 model, identifying underperforming areas. The evaluations primarily relied on the frequency of strategy utilization within the corresponding tasks.

In response to these findings, we further tested if the doctor's performances of the E5 were regulated by the patient's attitude or behaviors. Using the Stanford Topic Modeling Toolbox (Version 0.2.1), we identified four dimensions of patient attitude and behavior: 'Polite Patient Engagement' emerged through courteous patient communication and attempts to build rapport; 'Informed Patient Literacy' was evident in patients demonstrating an understanding of their health conditions and medication; 'Patient Anxiety Expression' captured instances where patients articulated their worries or fears; and 'Patient Trust and Adherence' was identified through patients' trust in medical advice and expressed intent to follow prescribed treatments. These dimensions served as variables in our subsequent analysis, enabling us to explore how patient attitudes and behaviors potentially influence doctors' efficacy in executing the E5 tasks. This bifocal analysis provides a nuanced understanding of the dynamics of doctor-patient interactions.

During the open coding process, two coders independently conducted the first round of coding. Subsequent coding iterations, guided by an iterative tagging approach, involved systematic revisions and refinements of the initial codes. Any coding discrepancies were resolved through case-by-case discussions, ensuring the emergence of nuanced themes that accurately represented the data. sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: Following an in-depth examination of our comprehensive dataset, we adapted the widely recognized E4 model [22] to accommodate the distinct context of Chinese online medical consultations by incorporating an additional factor -'Enable'. As depicted in Table 1, this refined E5 model presents a comprehensive framework with a range of practical strategies covering all five components, offering a more detailed framework for online doctor-patient interactions in the Chinese context.

The various communication strategies, presented in Table 1 and categorized according to the five tasks constituting the E5 model, serve as pivotal benchmarks for assessing doctors' performance in online consultations.

Specifically, the 'Engage' task is paramount to successful consultations as it bridges the gap between a physician's diagnostic approach and a patient's illness narrative. In our study, we pinpointed three specific strategies-addressing, greeting, and following up on symptoms-that strengthen this essential linage.

The 'Empathize' task involves understanding and verbalizing a patient's emotions, values, and experiences. This task encompasses three main components: acknowledging and recognizing the patient's feelings and values, validating these emotions, and appearing 'present' and 'with' the patient. In our dataset, we delineated three distinct strategies: inquiring about the patient's feelings, acknowledging these emotions,

paragraphs: The proposed extended E5 Model.

The E5 model Amount Frequency (%) Engage 317 63.4 addressing 12 2.6 greeting 257 51.4 following up on symptoms 180 36 Empathize 153 30.6 inquiring about the patient's feelings 4 0.8 acknowledging the patient's feelings 8 1.6 concern marker 148 29.6 Educate 150 30 explaining the rationale for the diagnosis 109 21.8 explaining the process and expected outcome 63 12.6 Enlist 451 90.2 making diagnostic recommendations 445 89 offering long-term care advice 56 11.2 Enable 178 35.6 sharing hospital-related information 140 28 guiding alternative access to care 50 10 Total 500 100

1 A consultation may be suspended if neither party responds. In most cases, it was usually the patient who didn't follow up with the consultation. 2 For more information on the Privacy Policy of the platform, one could visit the following official webpage: https://www.haodf.com/info/privacy_policy. php.

and using concern markers. 'Educate' constitutes a multifaceted dimension of patient education. It involves the physician understanding the patient's cognitive, emotional, and value frameworks, pre-existing health knowledge, and specific concerns. The doctor's role transcends mere information dissemination; it also involves assessing the patient's understanding level, bridging knowledge gaps, and rectifying misconceptions to align the patient's perspective with medical evidence. In our study, we identified two strategies: explaining the rationale behind the diagnosis and explaining the process and expected outcomes.

The 'Enlist' task aims to bolster patient autonomy by involving them in decision-making process and fostering adherence to medical advice, thereby augmenting their self-care capabilities. In this study, we recognized two strategies: making diagnostic recommendations and providing long-term care advice.

Lastly, the supplementary 'Enable' task, as identified in our dataset, entails the doctor offering additional assistance to the patient, either by guiding them through offline hospital navigation or advising on alternative avenues of care when direct hospital access is restricted. Practical strategies associated with this task include sharing hospital-related information and guiding alternative access to care. These strategies, aligned with the E5 model, offer a more nuanced lens for evaluating Chinese doctors' performance online and shed light on the digital healthcare communication dynamics.

Our findings disclose proficient execution of the 'Engage' and 'Enlist' tasks among Chinese doctors, contrasted with subpar performance in 'Empathize,' 'Educate,' and 'Enable' tasks. Table 2 illustrates how doctors' performance across the E5 model tasks fluctuates in accordance with distinct patient attitudes and behaviors. Notably, doctors adjusted their strategies based on patients' medical literacy, expression of anxiety, trust or adherence towards the doctor, and levels of polite engagement. These fluctuations highlight the dynamic interplay between doctor and patient, as well as the corresponding task execution within online medical consultations.

Table 3 delineates how doctors' execution of the E5 tasks varies based on the four dimensions of patient attitudes and behaviors. When patients exhibit 'informed patient literacy', characterized by a comprehensive understanding of medications, doctors' performance in the Engage task differs significantly from their general task performance. Similarly, in the 'patient anxiety expression' dimension, where patients express worries or anxiety, doctors' execution of Empathize and Educate exhibits marked differences. In scenarios characterized by 'patient trust and adherence', wherein patients express trust or a likelihood of complying with doctors' advice, doctors' competency in Engage, Empathize, Educate, and Enable tasks is notably affected. Lastly, in the 'polite patient engagement' dimension, where patients strive for rapport, doctors' performance on the Empathize, Educate, Enlist, and Enable tasks also presents substantial divergence.

sections.header_raw: On the necessity of the additional enable in Chinese context
sections.header_norm: on the necessity of the additional enable in chinese context
paragraphs: Utilizing a dataset of 500 dialogues from MedDialog-CN [52], our research demonstrates that doctors in Chinese online medical consultations do not solely adhere to the traditional tasks encapsulated in the well-established E4 model (Engage, Empathize, Educate, and Enlist). They also undertake an additional task 'Enable', as part of the broader E5 model. This component involves assisting patients in navigating both the digital and traditional healthcare environments, as well as offering guidance on alternative home-based treatments. Specifically, 'Enable' encompasses logistical support, such as directing patients to the relevant departments in physical healthcare facilities and clarifying hospital visitation procedures. It also extends to advising patients on home-based treatments, particularly when limitations like geographical constraints or quarantine measures restrict immediate access to offline medical services. Consequently, whereas the E4 model primarily addresses the emotional, relational, educational and diagnostical aspects of a consultation, the 'Enable' task zeroes in on the pragmatic nuances, equipping patients to navigate the healthcare system more effectively.

In developed countries such as the United States, non-physician healthcare personnel often manage the logistical aspects of healthcare, including appointment scheduling and departmental guidance [53,54]. Roles like medical secretaries, receptionists, nurses, and medical social workers serve as patient guides, thereby allowing physicians to concentrate on their core clinical responsibilities. Advanced healthcare infrastructures often employ additional resources like patient navigators and triage systems to optimize logistical management [55][56][57]. While physicians in these settings may occasionally assume responsibilities related to the 'Enable' task, particularly in complex cases, this task assumes heightened importance within the framework of Chinese online medical consultations [58].

Moreover, our dataset shows instances where the 'Enable' task extends beyond general guidance, with doctors assuming a more proactive role in assisting patients through systemic or situational impediments. For instance, doctors may provide specific advice to patients seeking care at high-tier public hospitals (e.g., 3 A level hospitals), including recommending nearby hospitals that facilitate insurance reimbursement [35]. In circumstances where mobility is restricted due to epidemic containment measures [59], doctors may counsel patients on liaising with local authorities for medical access or guide them in administering temporary home-based relief measures. These instances reveal an expanded role of the 'Enable' task, where doctors not only offer logistical support but also actively help patients navigate both systemic or situational healthcare barriers.

The 'Enable' task in our proposed E5 model directly addresses specific challenges endemic to the Chinese healthcare system. One notable challenge is the low physician-to-patient ratio, which places considerable strain on physicians and often results in abbreviated consultation times, thereby affecting the quality of care [60,61]. Moreover, China's tiered public hospital system often poses obstacles for rural patients in accessing high-quality medical services, thereby exacerbating existing health disparities [62,63]. Furthermore, the lack of a streamlined triage system often obliges patients to select their own department for consultations, a task that can be daunting for those unfamiliar with the healthcare system. Coupled with the intricate health insurance policies, patients face extra hurdles navigating the system [64]. Given these challenges, the 'Enable' task emerges as an indispensable component of online medical consultations in China. It allows doctors to offer the necessary logistical support, helping patients navigate the healthcare system and improving the effectiveness of healthcare delivery in the digital age [65,66]. Meanwhile, China's unique cultural context also significantly influences the shift from the E4 to the E5 model in online medical consultations. For instance, Chinese cultural norms, which prioritize respect for authority [67], lead patients to rely more heavily on doctors for logistical healthcare aspects, which are typically managed by non-physicians in other contexts [68]. This, in turn, heightens the importance of the 'Enable' task, as doctors frequently guide patients through the healthcare system. Additionally, cultural tendencies towards indirect communication [69,70] further complicate this dynamic. Such tendencies may cause patients to withhold logistical concerns, while physicians might feel pressured to accommodate all logistical requests. To address these complexities and uphold patient-centered care, physicians often provide detailed navigational and logistical support, thereby bridging understanding gaps and ensuring holistic healthcare.

Therefore, the extended E5 model serves as both an adaptation to the unique challenges of Chinese healthcare system and a cultural accommodation, effectively capturing the nuances of Chinese societal norms within the framework of online medical consultations.

While physicians in these settings may occasionally assume responsibilities related to the 'Enable' task, particularly in complex cases, this task assumes heightened importance within the framework of Chinese online medical consultations [58].

Moreover, our dataset shows instances where the 'Enable' task extends beyond general guidance, with doctors assuming a more proactive role in assisting patients through systemic or situational impediments. For instance, doctors may provide specific advice to patients seeking care at high-tier public hospitals (e.g., 3 A level hospitals), including recommending nearby hospitals that facilitate insurance reimbursement [35]. In circumstances where mobility is restricted due to epidemic containment measures [59], doctors may counsel patients on liaising with local authorities for medical access or guide them in administering temporary home-based relief measures. These instances reveal an expanded role of the 'Enable' task, where doctors not only offer logistical support but also actively help patients navigate both systemic or situational healthcare barriers.

The 'Enable' task in our proposed E5 model directly addresses specific challenges endemic to the Chinese healthcare system. One notable challenge is the low physician-to-patient ratio, which places considerable strain on physicians and often results in abbreviated consultation times, thereby affecting the quality of care [60,61]. Moreover, China's tiered public hospital system often poses obstacles for rural patients in accessing high-quality medical services, thereby exacerbating existing health disparities [62,63]. Furthermore, the lack of a streamlined triage system often obliges patients to select their own department for consultations, a task that can be daunting for those unfamiliar with the healthcare system. Coupled with the intricate health insurance policies, patients face extra hurdles navigating the system [64]. Given these challenges, the 'Enable' task emerges as an indispensable component of online medical consultations in China. It allows doctors to offer the necessary logistical support, helping patients navigate the healthcare system and improving the effectiveness of healthcare delivery in the digital age [65,66]. Meanwhile, China's unique cultural context also significantly influences the shift from the E4 to the E5 model in online medical consultations. For instance, Chinese cultural norms, which prioritize respect for authority [67], lead patients to rely more heavily on doctors for logistical healthcare aspects, which are typically managed by non-physicians in other contexts [68]. This, in turn, heightens the importance of the 'Enable' task, as doctors frequently guide patients through the healthcare system. Additionally, cultural tendencies towards indirect communication [69,70] further complicate this dynamic. Such tendencies may cause patients to withhold logistical concerns, while physicians might feel pressured to accommodate all logistical requests. To address these complexities and uphold patient-centered care, physicians often provide detailed navigational and logistical support, thereby bridging understanding gaps and ensuring holistic healthcare.

Therefore, the extended E5 model serves as both an adaptation to the unique challenges of Chinese healthcare system and a cultural accommodation, effectively capturing the nuances of Chinese societal norms within the framework of online medical consultations.
sections.preview_head: Utilizing a dataset of 500 dialogues from MedDialog-CN [52], our research demonstrates that doctors in Chinese online medical consultations do not solely adhere to the traditional tasks encapsulated in the well-established E4 model (Engage, Empathize, Educate, and Enlist). They also undertake an additional task 'Enable', as part of the broader E5 model. This component involves assisting patients in navigating both the digital and traditional healthcare environments, as well as offering guidance on alternative home-based treatments.
sections.preview_tail: Therefore, the extended E5 model serves as both an adaptation to the unique challenges of Chinese healthcare system and a cultural accommodation, effectively capturing the nuances of Chinese societal norms within the framework of online medical consultations.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
sections.header_raw: Evaluation of Chinese doctors' performance of the E5 model in online medical consultation
sections.header_norm: evaluation of chinese doctors performance of the e5 model in online medical consultation
paragraphs: Our analysis demonstrates an unbalanced implementation of the E5 model tasks in Chinese online medical consultations. Notably, while 'Enlist' and 'Engage' are effectively carried out, the performance of 'Enable,' 'Empathize,' and 'Educate' lags behind. These findings may suggest a focus on diagnostic-centric conversations among Chinese doctors, often at the expense of critical aspects like patient education, emotional support, and shared decision-making [71-73] [71-73].

Such a pattern suggests that the traditional paternalistic healthcare model, in which doctors largely control healthcare decisions [74] [74], may still predominate in China [73] [73]. Institutional factors, like the high patient-to-doctor ratio [35] [35], could exacerbate this issue, especially in time-constrained online settings. Additionally, the prevailing emphasis on technical over humanistic aspects of medicine could further intensify this trend [75] [75]. Consequently, tasks demanding deeper interaction and greater time investment-namely empathizing and educating-may be overlooked.

Nevertheless, the dataset offers encouraging insights into the reciprocal doctor-patient relationship, highlighting the impact of patient behaviors and attitudes on the effectiveness of the E5 model's tasks. For instance, factors like patient trust, adherence, literacy, expression of anxiety, and politeness significantly affect the doctors' execution of the tasks. This interplay underscores the dynamic nature of doctor-patient interactions and the potential for strategic patient behavior to enhance consultations. However, the current pattern of E5 tasks performance suggests a need for a paradigm shift towards a more balanced, patientcentered online consultation model. Bolstering public trust in healthcare professionals, a cornerstone of effective doctor-patient relationships [76], could be a promising starting point, as evidenced by our findings.

Future studies could explore methods to improve empathetic communication and patient education in online consultations. For example, the use of AI-driven empathy metrics or automated educational resources could amplify the 'human element' in virtual healthcare interactions. China's burgeoning digital healthcare landscape offers substantial potential; strategic refinements could transform online consultations into more patient-focused platforms, catering effectively to a large population.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: There are several limitations to our study that warrant consideration. First, the 500 dialogues from the 2020 MedDialog-CN dataset offer a snapshot rather than a complete picture of online medical consultations in China. Second, the study's cross-sectional focus on isolated interactions from 2020 might not capture evolving communication patterns. Lastly, while we provide comprehensive insights, our study does not evaluate the effectiveness of the consultations, such as patient satisfaction or treatment adherence, which could provide a more comprehensive evaluation of the communication models.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: This study introduces an E5 model to assess Chinese online medical consultation, shedding lights on variations in doctors' proficiency across the model's tasks and how these interact with different patient attitudes and behaviors. Our findings show that 'Enlist' is performed most frequently, while the tasks of 'Empathize', 'Educate', and 'Enable' are less commonly executed. This imbalance suggests a tilt toward a paternalistic healthcare approach, underscoring the need for specialized training programs to better integrate empathy and education in online healthcare. Furthermore, our investigation reveals that patient behaviors like trust and adherence significantly influence the tasks of 'Engage', 'Empathize', 'Educate', and 'Enable'. This highlights the dynamic and reciprocal nature of online doctor-patient interactions, suggesting a need for adaptive communication strategies to address varied patient behaviors effectively.

These findings elucidate the intricate dynamics prevalent in digital healthcare, offering invaluable insights for future research. By better understanding these dynamics, healthcare professionals can refine their communicative strategies and care delivery modalities, thereby fostering an environment conducive to patient-centered care. The study's outcomes thus contribute to the ongoing discourse on the optimization of online medical consultations, establishing a foundational basis for future research aimed at augmenting patient care in the digital healthcare era.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: Our assessment of the E5 model in Chinese online medical consultations prompts several practical recommendations. From the standpoint of the patient, our data substantiates a positive correlation between proactive patient engagement and the efficacy of online consultations, thereby underscoring the imperative of active patient involvement. A potential strategy to promote this involvement encompasses the fortification of public trust in healthcare practitioners and institutions through meticulously designed campaigns. Furthermore, the incorporation of elementary medical education into primary and secondary school curricula could serve as a cornerstone for enhancing patients' medical literacy. Collectively, these initiatives can facilitate more comprehensive and efficacious patient-doctor dialogues, thereby ameliorating healthcare outcomes in the long term.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: Shifting focus to physicians, our data identifies specific domains warranting further enhancement. One pivotal area is the strengthening of empathetic communication, achievable through continued professional development programs. By accentuating the significance of empathy, these programs could equip physicians with the requisite skills to discern and address patients' emotional states more effectively. This could improve the implementation of the 'Empathize' task and has the potential to augment patient satisfaction [91]. Additionally, our analysis indicates that building rapport-via strategies like personalized introductions, the use of authentic profile photos, and adherence to courteous formalities-can elevate physicians' initial patient interactions. The adoption of these measures has the potential to solidify initial doctor-patient relationships, thus potentially enhancing the overall efficacy of online consultations.

sections.paper_id: Wang and Wang - 2024 - Communication model in Chinese online medical consultations Insights and implications
paragraphs: From the standpoint of online platforms, our findings signal the crucial importance of integration and optimization. For instance, the efficacy of the 'Enable' task could be enhanced by integrating nonphysician healthcare personnel, such as medical social workers and patient navigators, into the online consultation workflow [77,78]. By taking on logistical roles, these professionals can alleviate the burden on physicians, thereby freeing them to engage in more complex biomedical discussions. Furthermore, platform refinements that include automated scheduling capabilities and integrated procedural guides for healthcare could streamline the consultation process, further alleviating the logistical pressure on physicians. Our findings indicate that such improvements could potentially amplify the efficacy of the 'Enable' task and optimize the patient-doctor interactions in online settings.

paragraphs: Finally, addressing the systemic challenges inherent in the Chinese healthcare system necessitates broader policy-level interventions. Our analysis underscores the neccessity for policy innovations that improve triage systems and mitigate healthcare access disparities between urban and rural areas [35]. Such measures would ameliorate various systemic issues, promoting more equitable healthcare provision. Collectively, these recommendations offer a holistic strategy to improve online medical consultations in China. They advocate for wider adoption of the E5 model and promote patient-centered care in the digital age, all based on the insights garnered from our study.
